step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.

Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. The stronger and more personal the reason, the more effective it is as a motivational anchor.

turns.user_message: well i smoke immediatly after i wake up . 30 min after i wake up
step2_rag_chunks: Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.
Morning cravings are common for people who smoke regularly. Drinking water, brushing your teeth, or going for a short walk can help reduce the urge to smoke.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You have enough understanding of this patient. Introduce ONE relevant coping strategy matched to their specific triggers and past attempts. Do NOT just name the strategy — briefly explain how to apply it in practice using the clinical knowledge (when to use it, what to do like a plan). Then check if the patient is open to trying it. Keep it simple, specific, and actionable.

Therapist:
step7_response: Given your immediate morning craving after waking up, let's try a technique called the "10-minute delay". When a craving hits, tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to, and then do something else for those 10 minutes. This can help the urge to smoke weaken significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up. Are you willing to try this technique when you wake up tomorrow?

step2_rag_chunks: Smoking is often tied to a habit loop â€” a cue triggers the urge, smoking is the routine, and relief is the reward. Understanding what your specific cues are is the first step. It could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or even a person. Once we identify the cue, we can work on changing just the routine while keeping the relief.

A craving is not a command â€” it is a wave. It builds, peaks, and fades, usually within 3 to 5 minutes. Urge surfing means observing the craving without acting on it â€” noticing where you feel it in your body, watching it get stronger, and then watching it pass. Each time you surf an urge without smoking, it gets slightly easier the next time.

Bidi smoke is more concentrated than cigarette smoke and delivers higher levels of nicotine despite being thinner. This means bidi dependence can be as strong as or stronger than cigarette dependence, and withdrawal symptoms can be equally intense. Standard cessation support â€” behavioral strategies and nicotine replacement â€” is just as applicable and effective for bidi smokers.

Keep it simple, specific, and actionable.
